findings: PayPal averages ~4.4% on cross-border transactions plus ~3-4% FX markup on currency conversion, totalling 7-10% on a typical international B2B order before any supplier-side cost is added
Wise personal account terms (Section 1.4): 'You may not use your personal Wise account to receive business payments.' Personal accounts are explicitly limited to personal purposes
~95% of Chinese suppliers transact domestically via Alipay or WeChat Pay; credit cards and PayPal sit at the edge of acceptance, mostly for samples or small first orders
Wise has tightened compliance: small business owners report payments frozen for invoice/contract proof; account closures cited for 'too high balance' or business volume on personal accounts
B2B-built tools (WF, Airwallex Business, OFX) include bulk payment workflows, beneficiary verification, FX forwards (3-12mo lock), and accounting export. Personal-tool apps stop at send-money UI

angles.video_title: Why your travel money app cracks at $20K
angles.hook: Your travel money app is doing $20K supplier payments. It wasn't designed for this.
angles.body: Personal apps were built for one user, one trip, one currency. Volume limits sit around $50K to $100K a year. Compliance dragnets pull random transfers for invoice review. There's no bulk pay, no FX forward, no accounting export. The product is doing the wrong job, well.
angles.close: Personal tool, business volume. Something has to give.
angles.visual: Split frame: holiday photo with travel app on the left, weekly $20K supplier wire list on the right. Same app icon. One breaks.

angles.video_title: The four things consumer apps don't have
angles.hook: If your payments tool doesn't have these four things, it's a consumer app.
angles.body: One: bulk pay. Twenty suppliers in one upload, not twenty single transfers. Two: beneficiary verification. Account name matched to business registration before money leaves. Three: FX forwards. Lock 3 months of CNY rate today. Four: accounting export. Reconciled rows into your books, not screenshots.
angles.close: You're not failing the tool. The tool was built for someone else.
angles.visual: Four checkbox rows fill in: bulk pay, supplier verification, FX forward, accounting export. Tick each one. End frame: 'This is a B2B tool.'

angles.video_title: Personal app limits caught up to you
angles.hook: Your personal account just got flagged for the third time this month.
angles.body: When the same login does holiday transfers and supplier wires, the platform's risk system can't tell them apart. So it freezes. It asks for invoices. It caps you at $50K a year. The product isn't broken. It's working as designed, for a different user.
angles.close: If business is moving through it, business tools should hold it.
angles.visual: Phone screen showing: 'Transfer paused. We need some checks.' Compliance email overlay. Same app, same user, two roles colliding.

angles.video_title: Why the same app costs you double the rate
angles.hook: Same app. Same currency pair. Two different rates.
angles.body: Personal tier and business tier on the same platform run different pricing. Personal: 0.5 to 1% on CNY. Business: tighter rate, plus FX forwards and bulk pay you can't access from the personal side. The platform isn't tricking you. You're on the wrong product.
angles.close: Check which tier you're on before you check the rate.
angles.visual: Two phones side by side, same brand app, same THB to CNY screen. Different rates highlighted. Caption: 'personal' vs 'business'.

findings: PayPal international B2B fees typically sit at ~4.4% transaction + 3-4% FX margin = ~7-8% total on a cross-border supplier payment. Industry estimates put a $10K order at $700-1000 in total cost
On $20K: ~$880 transaction fee (4.4%) + ~$700 FX margin (3.5%) = ~$1,580 visible cost, ~7.9% of payment value
Most Chinese suppliers are reluctant to accept PayPal and often add a price surcharge to absorb their own receiving fee. Some 'add 4-5%' to PI when PayPal is the rail
Supplier-side cost: PayPal charges receivers ~4.4% + fixed fee for cross-border commercial. Suppliers either eat it or pass it back
21-day fund holds and 180-day chargeback windows on PayPal create operational risk on the supplier side, which they price in

angles.video_title: What a $20K PayPal supplier payment actually costs
angles.hook: You sent $20,000 by PayPal. The receipt says $20,000. The real cost was higher.
angles.body: Cross-border transaction fee: 4.4%, that's $880. FX conversion margin to CNY: roughly 3.5%, another $700. Many Chinese suppliers absorb a receiving fee, so they quote you 4 to 5% higher on the PI. Add it up: visible cost $1,580, plus a quiet price uplift baked into your invoice.
angles.close: PayPal works. It's just priced for retail, not for $20K weekly orders.
angles.visual: Receipt-style breakdown stacking line by line. $20,000 at top. $880 added. $700 added. $800 supplier markup added. Bottom line: $22,380.

angles.video_title: PayPal fee receipt: line by line
angles.hook: Let's open the actual PayPal receipt on a $20K supplier payment.
angles.body: Line one: cross-border fee, around 4.4%. Line two: PayPal's FX rate, about 3.5% off mid-market. Line three: fixed transaction fee, 49 cents. Line four, invisible: the supplier may have raised the PI price 4 to 5% to cover their own PayPal receiving fee. The single transfer carries three or four layers of cost.
angles.close: One transfer. Four costs. Worth knowing before you pick the rail.
angles.visual: Animated receipt unfurls. Each fee line appears with a small icon. Final 'total cost' label at bottom in red.

angles.video_title: Why your supplier raised the PI 5%
angles.hook: You asked your supplier to accept PayPal. The next PI came back 5% higher.
angles.body: PayPal charges the receiver too. On a $20K order, your supplier loses roughly 4.4% just to access the money, plus a 21-day hold window. They cover it the only way they can: by repricing your next order. The cost didn't go away. It moved.
angles.close: Convenience for you can become a markup on you.
angles.visual: Two PI documents side by side. Old PI vs new PI. Unit price ticking up 5%. Note in supplier handwriting: 'PayPal fee included.'

angles.video_title: PayPal costs 4 to 8 percent. Here's where
angles.hook: PayPal isn't 2.9%. Not on a cross-border $20K payment.
angles.body: The 2.9% you remember is for domestic card payments. Cross-border B2B sits closer to 4.4% plus an FX margin of 3 to 4%. Total visible cost: 7 to 8% before your supplier's quiet uplift. On a $20K wire, that's $1,400 to $1,600 disappearing inside one transaction.
angles.close: The number depends on which PayPal you're using. Check before you send.
angles.visual: Big number ticker: 2.9% gets crossed out. Replaced by 4.4% + 3.5%. Final stacked total ~7.9%. Calculator UI vibe.

angles.video_title: PayPal at $200 vs PayPal at $20K
angles.hook: PayPal at $200 feels cheap. PayPal at $20,000 doesn't.
angles.body: At $200 the fixed-fee portion absorbs most of the pain. At $20,000 the percentage takes over. 4.4% on $200 is $9. 4.4% on $20,000 is $880, before FX. The product didn't change. The math did. Personal-volume apps are priced for personal volumes.
angles.close: If you wouldn't pay a bank $880, don't pay an app $880.
angles.visual: Two phone screens. Left: $200 transfer, $5.80 fee. Right: $20,000 transfer, $880 fee. Same UI. Same brand. Different reality.

findings: Only ~3-5% of Chinese suppliers accept credit cards directly; ~95% transact domestically via Alipay or WeChat Pay
Card transactions on Alibaba carry ~2.95-2.99% processing fee, capped at $12,000 per order, plus FX margin from the issuing bank (1-3%)
Suppliers reject cards primarily due to: 180-day chargeback window (high risk on already-shipped goods), 2-3% fee they'd absorb, and Alipay/WeChat being the standard domestic rail
Card workarounds: Trade Assurance (escrows the payment, supplier accepts because Alibaba holds the dispute risk), sourcing agents (agent takes card, settles to supplier in CNY)
Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B; most factories have never been set up to accept Visa or Mastercard

angles.video_title: Why your supplier said no to the card
angles.hook: You offered Visa. Your supplier asked for T/T. Here's why.
angles.body: Only about 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay domestically. Plus, a card transaction gives the buyer 180 days to dispute, on goods that already shipped. For the supplier, that's not payment. That's a loan with a return policy.
angles.close: The card isn't the problem. The category was built for retail, not factory orders.
angles.visual: Map of China overlaid with payment icons: Alipay everywhere, WeChat everywhere, tiny Visa logos in maybe 3 cities. Caption: 3 to 5% acceptance.

angles.video_title: Cards work for buyers. Not for sellers in China
angles.hook: A credit card is a buyer's tool. That's the problem.
angles.body: Cards exist to protect the buyer: chargeback, dispute, fraud reversal. All risk lives with the merchant. A Chinese factory selling $20K of goods on a 30 day production cycle can't carry 180 days of chargeback risk on 2% margins. They say no. It's not about you. It's about how cards are built.
angles.close: The rail matters. Cards aren't a supplier rail.
angles.visual: Card slid across a table, factory owner shaking head. Side-by-side: buyer protections list (long) vs seller protections (short). Imbalance is the visual.

angles.video_title: What 2.95% on a card actually buys you
angles.hook: Paying suppliers by card sounds simple. The 2.95% has layers.
angles.body: Alibaba's card fee is 2.95 to 2.99% on the payment, capped at $12,000 per order. Your issuing bank adds 1 to 3% FX margin. Some suppliers pass back another 2 to 4% to absorb their side. So the 'card convenience' tier sits around 6 to 8% all in, with a $12K ceiling per order.
angles.close: Cards are fine for samples. They get expensive at scale.
angles.visual: Stacked bar chart: 2.95% Alibaba + 2% FX + 3% supplier surcharge = ~8% total. Each layer fades in. $12K cap shown as a wall.

angles.video_title: Cards have no rail to a Chinese factory
angles.hook: There is no Visa terminal in a Foshan factory.
angles.body: When you 'pay by card', someone in the middle, often Alibaba or a sourcing agent, takes the card on their side and settles to the supplier in CNY via bank transfer or Alipay. The card never actually reached the supplier. You're paying a middleman for the bridge. The bridge is the cost.
angles.close: The card isn't talking to the factory. Something else is.
angles.visual: Animated flow: your card to Alibaba/agent to T/T to supplier. Highlight the middleman node. Caption: 'this layer is the fee'.

findings: Major bank FX margin on consumer transfers sits around 2-4% off mid-market; specialist business platforms typically run 0.3-1% on the same pair
On a $20K supplier wire at 2% personal vs 0.5% business FX spread = $400 vs $100. The 'spread' alone is $300 in your favour on one transfer
Mid-market rate = the rate banks use among themselves. Personal tools and bank apps quote a 'retail rate' that already has the margin baked in
For an SME doing $50K/month in CNY payments, a 1.5% spread difference = $750/mo = $9,000/yr disappearing inside the rate, never appearing on a line item
Personal accounts on the same provider as a business account often pay a different rate. Personal tier sees retail spread; business tier sees institutional spread

angles.video_title: The rate you see isn't the rate that exists
angles.hook: Open Google. Type 'THB to CNY'. That rate doesn't exist for your wallet.
angles.body: Google shows the mid-market rate. Banks use it among themselves. Your wallet sees a 'retail' rate, which already has 2 to 4% baked in. That's the spread. It never appears as a line item. It just shows up as 'this conversion came out lower than I expected'.
angles.close: The rate you see and the rate you get aren't the same currency.
angles.visual: Google search bar typing 'THB to CNY'. Mid-market rate displayed big. Then crossfade to wallet UI showing the worse rate. Difference highlighted.

angles.video_title: Personal vs business FX: the same currency, two prices
angles.hook: Same currency pair. Same day. Two different rates. Why?
angles.body: Personal-tier FX on most platforms runs 1.5 to 2% off mid-market. Business-tier runs 0.3 to 1%. That gap is the spread. On a $20K supplier wire, 1.5% of difference is $300. Times four weeks: $1,200 a month gone, inside the rate, no line item, no notification.
angles.close: If you're paying suppliers weekly, the tier change pays for itself in one transfer.
angles.visual: Two FX rate cards. Personal: 0.245 THB/CNY. Business: 0.247. Tiny gap. Annotated: 'this gap = $1,200/mo on $20K weekly'.

angles.video_title: A 1.5% spread on $50K/month is $9,000 a year
angles.hook: 1.5% sounds tiny. On supplier volumes, it isn't.
angles.body: An SME doing $50,000 a month in CNY supplier payments at a 1.5% spread loses $750 a month. That's $9,000 a year. Inside the FX rate. Nowhere on any invoice. Nowhere in your accounting export. You can't see it unless you compare your conversion rate to the mid-market rate on the same day.
angles.close: It's not a fee. It's the gap. The gap is real money.
angles.visual: Calculator UI. $50,000 × 1.5% × 12 = $9,000. Number scales up dramatically. Bottom caption: 'inside the rate, invisible'.

angles.video_title: How to spot the spread on any transfer
angles.hook: You can check the FX spread on any transfer in 10 seconds.
angles.body: Step one: look at the rate your app quoted you, say 0.245 THB to CNY. Step two: Google 'THB to CNY' on the same day, get the mid-market, say 0.249. Step three: divide the gap by mid-market: 0.004 over 0.249 = 1.6%. That's your spread. Multiply by your transfer to see what you actually paid.
angles.close: If you've never done this, do it on your last wire. The number will surprise you.
angles.visual: Three quick steps shown as phone screen recordings. App rate, Google rate, calculator. Final number: '1.6% spread' highlighted.

angles.video_title: Why retail FX exists at all
angles.hook: There's no law that says banks should charge you 2% to convert money.
angles.body: The spread exists because retail FX is a profit centre, not an infrastructure service. Banks set retail rates based on what they think you'll accept, not what the currency actually moves at. Business-tier exists because volume customers know what mid-market is and walk if you mark it up. Information gap is the entire product.
angles.close: If you know the mid-market rate, you stop being the retail customer.
angles.visual: Two windows: 'retail FX desk' (no questions asked) vs 'institutional desk' (visible mid-market screen). Same building, two doors.

findings: Cards work cleanly for: samples and small first orders under ~$1,000, agent payments, and Alibaba Trade Assurance orders where escrow holds the dispute risk
Alibaba Trade Assurance card cap: ~$12,000 per order. Above that, you're back on T/T or specialist rails
Card via sourcing agent: agent accepts your card on their side, settles to factory in CNY via Alipay or T/T. Agent takes a fee (typically 3-5%) for the bridge
Cards do NOT work for: bulk production orders >$12K, factories without Trade Assurance, ongoing weekly volumes, FX-locked payments, or any scenario where the supplier needs CNY directly
Buyer-side protection via card chargeback is the actual reason to use a card for samples: 180-day window catches bad samples that T/T can't recover

angles.video_title: When CAN you pay a Chinese supplier by card?
angles.hook: Cards don't always fail with Chinese suppliers. They work in three scenarios.
angles.body: One: samples and small first orders under $1,000, where the 2.95% fee is small and chargeback protects you. Two: Alibaba Trade Assurance orders up to $12K, where Alibaba escrows the money. Three: through a sourcing agent, who takes the card and settles to the factory in CNY. Outside those three, T/T or a B2B rail is the path.
angles.close: Cards have a use. Just know which one.
angles.visual: Three card icons appear with green checkmarks: samples, Trade Assurance, agent. Then a fourth (bulk production) appears with red X.

angles.video_title: The $12,000 wall on credit cards
angles.hook: Alibaba card payments cap at $12,000 per order. That's not arbitrary.
angles.body: Above $12K, the chargeback risk on a single order is too large for Alibaba to escrow against the supplier. So they push you to T/T. The $12K ceiling is roughly where samples become production. Cards live below the wall. Production orders live above it. Same factory, different rail.
angles.close: Cards are a sample tool. Above $12K, it's a different game.
angles.visual: Bar chart with a hard ceiling drawn at $12,000. Sample orders below the line in green. Production orders above in grey, with arrow pointing 'T/T zone'.

angles.video_title: Why cards are perfect for sample orders
angles.hook: For a $500 sample order, the credit card is doing real work.
angles.body: Sample order risk is high: you don't know if the factory is real, if quality matches the listing, if it ships at all. A card gives you a 180 day chargeback window. If the sample's wrong, you dispute. T/T at $500 has no recovery. The 2.95% fee on $500 is $15. Cheap insurance.
angles.close: Use the card where chargeback protection is the actual product.
angles.visual: Sample box arriving, quality check fails, chargeback button pressed, money returns. Compare to T/T scenario where money is just gone.

angles.video_title: The sourcing agent card trick
angles.hook: Your factory doesn't take cards. Your sourcing agent does.
angles.body: Most sourcing agents in Yiwu, Shenzhen, Guangzhou take Visa or Mastercard. You pay the agent by card. They settle to the factory in CNY by Alipay or T/T. The agent's fee is typically 3 to 5%. Add card fee plus agent fee and you're at 6 to 8%. Worth it for one-off orders. Expensive at weekly cadence.
angles.close: Useful escape hatch. Not a long-term rail.
angles.visual: Flow chart: you to agent (card icon) to factory (Alipay icon). Annotated fees: 2.95% + 3-5% = 6-8% total. Caption: 'escape hatch'.

angles.video_title: Three times the card was the wrong tool
angles.hook: Three real scenarios where the card was the wrong choice.
angles.body: One: $40K production order, hit the $12K Alibaba cap, had to split across four cards, paid 2.95% on every one. Two: weekly $8K orders, accumulated 3% card fee plus 3% FX = $480 a week, $25K a year. Three: factory wanted CNY directly, no Trade Assurance, card never reached them, paid an agent 5% to bridge. Same order, three different cost stacks.
angles.close: Cards have a slot. Knowing the slot saves the spend.
angles.visual: Three mini case cards stacked, each with the dollar amount of waste highlighted in red. End with: 'card is a tool, not the tool'.
